study: Attexis- digital therapy for ADHD in adulthood [NCT06221930, PMID:41810578]
gt_key_outcomes_findings: Significant reduction in ADHD symptoms (primary: ASRS score decrease 11.6% points in intervention vs. ~4.4 in control; p<0.001; effect size 0.65); improved impulsivity (BIS-11 ~15% reduction; p<0.01) and well-being (WHO-5 increase ~10 points; p<0.05); high adherence (~75%); clinically meaningful improvements exceeding MCID for ASRS (>9 points).
gpt-4o__key_outcomes_findings: Significantly lower ADHD symptom severity in the intervention group at 3 months (baseline-adjusted mean difference = -5.0 points; d = 0.85, p < .001). Significant improvements across all secondary outcomes, effects stable at 6 months. Responder analyses confirmed clinical relevance. Consistent effects across sex, medication use, psychotherapy status, and treatment changes. No adverse events reported.
gemini-3.1-pro-preview__key_outcomes_fin: Intent-to-treat analyses showed significantly lower ADHD symptom severity in the intervention group at 3 months (baseline-adjusted mean difference = -5.0 points; d = 0.85, p < .001). Significant improvements were also observed across all secondary outcomes, and effects remained stable at 6 months. Responder analyses confirmed clinical relevance. Subgroup analyses demonstrated consistent effects across sex, medication use, psychotherapy status, and treatment changes. No adverse events related to attexis were reported.
claude-sonnet-4-6__key_outcomes_findings: Intent-to-treat analyses showed significantly lower ADHD symptom severity in the intervention group at T1 (baseline-adjusted mean difference = -5.0 points; d = 0.85, p < .001). Significant improvements were also observed across all secondary outcomes (functional impairment, depressive symptoms, self-esteem, health-related quality of life). Effects remained stable at T2 (6 months). Responder analyses confirmed clinical relevance. Subgroup analyses demonstrated consistent effects across sex, medication use, psychotherapy status, and treatment changes. No adverse events related to attexis were reported.

gt_key_outcomes_findings: Significant symptom reduction (primary: IBS-SSS decrease ~100 points in intervention vs. ~30 in control; p<0.001; effect size ~0.6); improved QoL (IBS-QoL increase ~20 points; p<0.01), work/activity impairment (WPAI-IBS reduction ~15%; p<0.05), and health literacy (HLS-EU-Q16 improvement ~10%; p<0.05). High adherence (~80%); four recognized care effects (health status, QoL, coping, literacy); clinically meaningful per MCID (>50 points IBS-SSS).

Pain (NPRS 0–10): app group improved 1.5 points more than control (p < 0.001), Knee function (Kujala Score): app group significantly better than control (p < 0.05)
Both primary endpoints met criteria for clinical relevance and statistical superiority
Significant fatigue reduction (Chalder Fatigue Scale: –2.74 points vs. control at week 12, d=0.53; sustained at 24 weeks, p<0.001). Improved quality of life, daily functioning, and fatigue coping
gpt-4o__key_outcomes_findings: Significantly greater reductions in Chalder Fatigue Scale scores were observed in the ELEVIDA group at week 12 (between-group mean difference 2.74 points; 95% CI 1.16 to 4.32; p=0.0007; effect size d=0.53), with effects sustained at week 24 (between-group mean difference 2.19 points; 95% CI 0.57 to 3.82; p=0.0080).
gemini-3.1-pro-preview__key_outcomes_fin: Compared with the control group, significantly greater reductions in Chalder Fatigue Scale scores were seen in the ELEVIDA group at week 12 (primary endpoint, intention-to-treat analysis: between-group mean difference 2.74 points; 95% CI 1.16 to 4.32; p=0.0007; effect size d=0.53). These effects were sustained at week 24 (intention-to-treat analysis: between-group mean difference 2.19 points; 95% CI 0.57 to 3.82; p=0.0080).
claude-sonnet-4-6__key_outcomes_findings: At week 12 (primary endpoint), the ELEVIDA group showed significantly greater reductions in Chalder Fatigue Scale scores compared to control (intention-to-treat: between-group mean difference 2.74 points; 95% CI 1.16 to 4.32; p=0.0007; effect size d=0.53). Effects were sustained at week 24 (intention-to-treat: between-group mean difference 2.19 points; 95% CI 0.57 to 3.82; p=0.0080). The trial provides evidence for the effectiveness of a self-guided internet-based intervention to reduce fatigue in MS.

gt_key_outcomes_findings: The guided ACTonPain group showed significantly less pain interference than the control group in the ITT analysis (p = 0.01), with a moderate effect size at T1 and T2 (d = 0.58 respectively), corresponding to a number needed to treat (NNT) of 3.14 for both time points. Participants in the guided ACTonPain group also indicated higher pain acceptance (T1: d = 0.59; T2: d = 0.76). The unguided ACTonPain group showed to be significantly less depressed in comparison to the control group at at time T2 (d = 0.50). No significant differences with respect to effectiveness were found between the two ACTonPain groups (p>0.05).

At 12 weeks, the patients in the intervention group exhibited significantly lower pain interference scores (d = 0.69; 95% confidence interval: [0.46; 0.92]) than those in the control group; they also had fewer depressive symptoms (d = 0.40; [0.17; 0.63]) and experienced better health-related quality of life (d = -0.23; [-0.40; -0.06). These effects were still present at 6 and 12 months.

study: HelloBetter Diabetes [DRKS00004748, PMID:24238346, PMID:25710923, PMID:31218230, DOI:10.1111/dme.13173]
gt_key_outcomes_findings: Effectiveness: GET.ON M.E.D. was effective in reducing symptoms of depression over 2 and 6 months compared to the active control group.  Sustained effect: 45% of participants showed no clinically relevant symptoms at 6 months post-randomization. Economic: The intervention has a high probability of being cost-effective compared with the active psychoeducation control group, especially from a cost-effectiveness perspective.  Incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) and incremental cost-utility ratio (ICUR) were calculated using non-parametric bootstrap resampling techniques (2500 replications) over a 6-month period.

study: Kaia back pain - back exercises for home [DRKS00016329, PMID:31304380]
gt_key_outcomes_findings: At 12-week follow-up the Kaia App group reported significantly lower pain intensity (M = 2.70 (SD = 1.51)) compared to the control group (M = 3.40 (SD = 1.63)). Our results indicate that the Kaia App as a multidisciplinary back pain app is an effective treatment in LBP patients and is superior to physiotherapy in combination with online education.

Intention-to-treat analyses indicated that treatment was associated with greater reduction in self-reported symptoms of depression (effect size d = .80) and 12 times greater likelihood of experiencing at least 50% symptom improvement compared with waitlist control. Similar effects were observed for several secondary outcomes, such as interviewer-rated depression symptoms, well-being, and depression-related disability. Treatment effects for symptoms of social anxiety, panic, and traumatic intrusions were relatively small.

gt_key_outcomes_findings: Both arms improved over 12 weeks, but TAU-PLUS showed superior results (modified intention-to-treat analysis): - Primary (BDI-II): Greater symptom reduction in TAU-PLUS (-16.2 vs. -10.5 points; between-group difference: -5.7 points, p=0.03, Cohen's d=-0.73 [medium effect]). - Secondary: No significant differences in HAM-D (p=0.12) or WHO-5 (p=0.08); higher daily activity in TAU-PLUS (p=0.04, d=0.70); shorter inpatient stay (14 vs. 21 days, p=0.003); lower post-discharge re-admissions (8% vs. 24%, p=0.04) and care utilization (19% vs. 42%, p=0.01). - Other: Working alliance with Deprexis (but not therapists) predicted better MDD improvement and well-being; no adverse effects noted. Limitations: Small sample, ~20% dropout.
gpt-4o__key_outcomes_findings: At week 12, TAU-PLUS patients showed significant improvement in BDI-II scores (p=.03, d=-.73), greater daily activity (p=.04, d=.70), earlier discharge (p=.003), lower rate of post-hospital care (p=.01), and fewer re-admissions (p=.04). Secondary outcomes and alliance with therapists showed no significant differences between groups.
gemini-3.1-pro-preview__key_outcomes_fin: At week 12, BDI-II scores showed significant between-group differences favoring TAU-PLUS (p=.03, d=-.73, 95% CI -1.4 to .06). TAU-PLUS patients showed greater daily activity (p=.04, d=.70, 95% CI -.03 to 1.38) and were discharged significantly earlier (p=.003). Post-discharge, TAU-PLUS reported lower rates of post-hospital care (p=.01) and re-admissions (p=.04). Secondary outcome measures (including therapist alliance) were not significantly different between groups at endpoint. Working-alliance with deprexis significantly predicted MDD-improvement and wellbeing.
claude-sonnet-4-6__key_outcomes_findings: At week 12, modified ITT analyses showed significant between-group differences in BDI-II scores in favor of TAU-PLUS (p=.03), corresponding to a medium effect size (d=-.73, 95% CI -1.4 to .06). TAU-PLUS patients showed greater daily activity (p=.04, d=.70, 95% CI -.03 to 1.38) and were discharged significantly earlier from inpatient treatment (p=.003). Post-discharge, the TAU-PLUS group reported a lower rate of post-hospital care (p=.01) and re-admissions (p=.04). Secondary outcome measures including therapist alliance were not significantly different between groups at study endpoint. The patients' working alliance with deprexis significantly predicted MDD improvement and wellbeing. TAU plus deprexis was superior to TAU in improving subjective depression severity and daily activity.

gt_key_outcomes_findings: Of 396 participants, 216 (55%) completed post-assessment. Available case analyses: Significant BDI reduction (Cohen's d=0.64, 95% CI 0.33–0.94) and WSA improvement (d=0.64, 95% CI 0.33–0.95) favoring immediate-treatment. Intention-to-treat: BDI d=0.30 (95% CI 0.05–0.55); WSA d=0.36 (95% CI 0.10–0.61). Intervention group had higher odds of reliable symptom reduction (OR=6.8, 95% CI 2.90–18.19) and recovery (OR=17.3, 95% CI 2.3–130). Effects maintained at 6-month follow-up. 83% of users rated Deprexis as helpful; average 5.1 modules completed.
gpt-4o__key_outcomes_findings: Significant reduction in depression severity (BDI) with Cohen's d = .64 (CI 95% = 0.33 - 0.94) and significant improvement in social functioning (WSA) with Cohen's d = .64 (CI 95% = 0.33 - 0.95) at 9 weeks. Improvements were maintained at 6-month follow-up. Intention-to-treat analyses showed significant effects on depression (BDI: Cohen's d = .30, CI 95% = 0.05 - 0.55) and social functioning (WSA: Cohen's d = .36, CI 95% = 0.10 - 0.61). Higher percentage of intervention group experienced significant reduction in depression symptoms (BDI: OR = 6.8, CI 95% = 2.90 - 18.19) and recovery (OR = 17.3, CI 95% = 2.3 - 130). Over 80% of users found the program helpful.

Available case analyses revealed a significant reduction in depression severity (BDI), Cohen's d = .64 (CI 95% = 0.33 - 0.94), and significant improvement in social functioning (WSA), Cohen's d = .64 (CI 95% = 0.33 - 0.95). These improvements were maintained at 6-month follow-up. Intention-to-treat analyses confirmed significant effects on depression and social functioning improvements (BDI: Cohen's d = .30, CI 95% = 0.05 - 0.55; WSA: Cohen's d = .36, CI 95% = 0.10 - 0.61). A much higher percentage of patients in the intervention group experienced a significant reduction of depression symptoms (BDI: odds ratio [OR] = 6.8, CI 95% = 2.90 - 18.19) and recovered more often (OR = 17.3, 95% CI 2.3 - 130). More than 80% of the users felt subjectively that the program had been helpful.

gt_key_outcomes_findings: Results for the intention-to-treat (ITT) sample showed significant intervention effects on depression reduction between baseline and post-treatment (linear mixed model [MM], F1,155.6 = 9.00, p < .01, for the time by condition interaction), with a medium between-group effect size, Cohen's d = 0.57 (95% CI: 0.22–0.92). Group differences in depression severity at follow-up were marginally significant in the ITT sample, t (119) = 1.83, p = 0.07, and smaller than at post-treatment (PHQ-9, d = 0.33, 95% CI: − 0.03–0.69).
gpt-4o__key_outcomes_findings: Internet interventions for depression (Deprexis) are effective in the short term and exert long-term effects. Effects are likely to impact a small subset of problems, with patients reporting these problems benefiting more from the intervention. The configuration of direct and indirect effects replicates findings from another study examining the same intervention.
gemini-3.1-pro-preview__key_outcomes_fin: Item-level analysis showed that scale-level effects can be explained by small item-level effects on most depressive symptoms at all points of assessment. Higher scores on these items at baseline predicted overall symptom reduction throughout the whole assessment period. Deprexis directly affected certain symptoms of depression (e.g., worthlessness and fatigue) and certain aspects of the quality of life (e.g., overall impairment through emotional problems), while other domains were affected indirectly (e.g., depressed mood and concentration as well as activity level).
claude-sonnet-4-6__key_outcomes_findings: Item-level analysis showed that scale-level effects can be explained by small item-level effects on most depressive symptoms at all assessment points. Higher baseline scores on certain items predicted overall symptom reduction throughout the entire assessment period. Network intervention analysis revealed that Deprexis directly affected certain symptoms (e.g., worthlessness and fatigue) and certain quality of life aspects (e.g., overall impairment through emotional problems), while other domains were affected indirectly (e.g., depressed mood, concentration, and activity level). The configuration of direct and indirect effects replicated findings from a previous study examining the same intervention. The intervention demonstrated both short-term and long-term effects over 36 months.

gt_key_outcomes_findings: Primary Outcome (PHQ-9 Depression Severity): Significant reduction in self-reported depression symptoms post-treatment (3 months); clinically meaningful 5-point drop observed in intervention group vs. control. Good reliability (α=0.83). Secondary Outcomes (Clinician-Rated): HDRS-24: Moderate improvement in depression severity (α=0.79); high interrater reliability (r=0.92–0.96). QIDS-C16: Notable symptom reduction (α=0.75); diagnoses via MINI confirmed MDD in most participants. Suicidality: Low incidence; no intervention-related increases. Quality of Life & Progress: SF-12: Improvements in mental health subscale (norm M=50); physical health stable. FEP-2: Enhanced well-being and interpersonal functioning. Safety/Negative Effects: Minimal worsening on PHQ-9; no significant suicidality spikes.
gpt-4o__key_outcomes_findings: Changes in PHQ-9 from baseline differed significantly between groups (t825 = 6.12, p < 0.001). Post-assessment between-group effect size was d = 0.39 (95% CI: 0.13-0.64), stable at follow-up with d = 0.32 (95% CI: 0.06-0.69). Rate of participants experiencing minimally clinically important PHQ-9 change at post-assessment was higher in the intervention group (35.6% vs. 20.2%), with a number needed to treat of 7 (95% CI: 5-10).
gemini-3.1-pro-preview__key_outcomes_fin: Changes in PHQ-9 from baseline differed significantly between groups (t825 = 6.12, p < 0.001). The post-assessment between-group effect size in favour of the intervention was d = 0.39 (95% CI: 0.13-0.64) and was stable at follow-up with d = 0.32 (95% CI: 0.06-0.69). The rate of participants experiencing at least minimally clinically important PHQ-9 change at post-assessment was higher in the intervention group (35.6% vs. 20.2%) with a number needed to treat of 7 (95% CI: 5-10).
claude-sonnet-4-6__key_outcomes_findings: Changes in PHQ-9 from baseline differed significantly between groups (t825 = 6.12, p < 0.001 for the main effect of group). Post-assessment between-group effect size in favour of the intervention: d = 0.39 (95% CI: 0.13–0.64). Effect was stable at 6-month follow-up: d = 0.32 (95% CI: 0.06–0.69). Rate of participants experiencing at least minimally clinically important PHQ-9 change at post-assessment was higher in the intervention group (35.6% vs. 20.2%), with a number needed to treat of 7 (95% CI: 5–10). The Internet intervention was superior to CAU alone in reducing mild to moderate depressive symptoms.

gt_key_outcomes_findings:
At posttreatment, the use of dCBT-I was associated with a large reduction in the ISI (Diffadj = –7.60) in comparison to WLC (d = –2.08). This clinical improvement was also reflected in responder and remission rates. Treatment effects were also observed for daytime functioning, quality of life, symptoms of depression and anxiety (ds = 0.26–1.02) and at long-term follow-up (intervention group only; ds = 0.18–1.65). No effects were found for dream and nightmare frequency.  From overall n = 238 participants, n = 88 scored ≥ 18 in the ADS-K
(dCBT-I= 40; waitlist-control [WLC]= 48) and n = 120 scored ≥ 46
in the STAI-T (dCBT-I= 53, WLC= 67) at baseline, and were conse-
quently assigned to the depression and anxiety subgroups, respec-
tively.
gpt-4o__key_outcomes_findings: At post-treatment, dCBT-I was associated with a large reduction in ISI scores (Diffadj = -7.60) compared to the control group (d = -2.08). Clinical improvements were observed in responder and remission rates, daytime functioning, quality of life, depression, and anxiety (effect sizes d = 0.26–1.02). Long-term effects were sustained in the intervention group (effect sizes d = 0.18–1.65). No effects were found for dream and nightmare frequency.
gemini-3.1-pro-preview__key_outcomes_fin: At posttreatment (8 weeks), dCBT-I was associated with a large reduction in the ISI (adjusted difference = -7.60) compared to the waitlist control (d = -2.08). Clinical improvement was reflected in responder and remission rates. Treatment effects were also observed for daytime functioning, quality of life, symptoms of depression and anxiety (ds = 0.26-1.02), and at long-term follow-up for the intervention group (ds = 0.18-1.65). No effects were found for dream and nightmare frequency.
claude-sonnet-4-6__key_outcomes_findings: N = 238 participants (67.6% female, age range 19–81 years); n = 118 randomized to dCBT-I, n = 120 to waitlist control. At post-treatment (8 weeks), dCBT-I was associated with a large reduction in ISI (Diffadj = –7.60) compared to waitlist control (effect size d = –2.08). Clinical improvement was reflected in responder and remission rates. Treatment effects were also observed for daytime functioning, quality of life, depression, and anxiety symptoms (ds = 0.26–1.02). Long-term follow-up effects (intervention group only) were sustained (ds = 0.18–1.65). No effects were found for dream and nightmare frequency. Conclusion: dCBT-I reduces insomnia symptoms and improves daytime functioning in a heterogeneous insomnia population in Germany with sustained long-term effects.

gt_key_outcomes_findings:
Between-group comparisons revealed large treatment effects at
8 ( p < 0.001; d= 1.08) and 16 weeks ( p= 0.003, d= 0.87) in favour
of the digital CBT-I group. On average, ISI scores were reduced by
3.70 and 2.97 points, in comparison to digital sleep monitoring
gpt-4o__key_outcomes_findings: Large treatment effects (d = 0.87–1.08) were observed, indicating robust reductions (-3.70 and -2.97, respectively; p ≤ 0.003) in insomnia severity in the digital CBT-I arm compared to the control group. Improvements in self-reported and actigraphy-derived sleep continuity variables were also noted.
gemini-3.1-pro-preview__key_outcomes_fin: At 8- and 16-weeks post-randomisation, large treatment effects (d = 0.87–1.08) indicated robust reductions (-3.70 and -2.97, respectively; p ≤ 0.003) in insomnia severity in the digital cognitive behavioural therapy arm, relative to digital sleep monitoring. Treatment effects in favour of digital cognitive behavioural therapy were also found for self-reported and actigraphy-derived sleep continuity variables, indicating that sleep improved throughout the 8-week intervention period.
claude-sonnet-4-6__key_outcomes_findings: 56 participants (48 females, mean age 45.55 ± 13.70 years) were randomized: dCBT-I (n=29) vs. digital sleep monitoring (n=27). At 8 weeks post-randomization, large treatment effects (d = 0.87–1.08) indicated robust reductions in insomnia severity in the dCBT-I arm relative to digital sleep monitoring (-3.70 points on ISI; p ≤ 0.003). At 16 weeks post-randomization, reductions were -2.97 points on ISI (p ≤ 0.003). Treatment effects in favour of dCBT-I were also found for self-reported and actigraphy-derived sleep continuity variables, indicating sleep improved throughout the 8-week intervention period. Linear mixed models were used to assess between-group differences. The study concluded that dCBT-I achieves clinical improvements for patients with insomnia, supporting the specific effects of CBT-I.

claude-sonnet-4-6__key_outcomes_findings: A total of 140 participants were randomized (dCBT-I n=70, WLC n=70; 120 women, mean age 39.76 ± 11.65 years). Large treatment effects at 12 and 24 weeks showed significant reductions in depressive symptoms (PHQ-9) in the dCBT-I group compared to WLC (-3.34 and -2.83 respectively; ps <0.001; Cohen's d = 0.66–0.78). Treatment effects in favor of dCBT-I were also found for insomnia severity (ds = 1.46–1.94) and most secondary outcomes (ds = 0.33–1.14). Effects remained stable across the heterogeneous sample.

gt_key_outcomes_findings: The dCBT-I group showed large improvements in the severity of insomnia compared to the WLC both after 8 (−4.36, p < 0.001; d = −1.18) and 24 weeks (−4.88, p < 0.001; d = −1.32). Pain-related impairments and life control also improved, favoring dCBT-I (ds = 0.22–0.35). Moderate-to-large treatment effects were also observed for secondary outcomes, including daytime sleepiness, fatigue, and dysfunctional beliefs and attitudes about sleep (ds = 0.47–1.12).

gt_key_outcomes_findings:
The clinical trial was able to show positive outcomes on the KOOS Pain and Symptoms subscale of the primary endpoint KOOS in the interventional group compared to the standard of care group. A statistically significant pain reduction could be seen at t1 (p = 0.0005), t2 (p = 0.0068) and t4 (approximately 6 weeks after surgery; p = 0.01). A significant
reduction of symptoms at t1 (p = 0.0001), t2 (p = 0.0036) and t6 (p = 0.0377) were recognized. These findings are further supported by the significant benefit of the pain NRS at t4 and positive tendencies at t3, t5 and t6. The KOOS subscales quality of life (p = 0.0066), activities of daily living (p = 0.0009) and Sport (p = 0.0484) showed significant effects before
surgery.

A statistically significant pain reduction was seen at t1 (p = 0.0005), t2 (p = 0.0068), and t4 (approximately 6 weeks after surgery; p = 0.01). A significant reduction of symptoms was found at t1 (p = 0.0001), t2 (p = 0.0036), and t6 (p = 0.0377). These findings were further supported by significant benefit on the pain NRS at t4 and positive tendencies at t3, t5, and t6. The KOOS subscales quality of life (p = 0.0066), activities of daily living (p = 0.0009), and Sport (p = 0.0484) showed significant effects before surgery.

Also in relation to the subscale "Activities of Daily Life (ADL)" of the KOOS, there was a statistically significant improvement of 12.0 (adjusted mean difference) in the IG compared to the KG.

gpt-4o__key_outcomes_findings: Significant improvement on UIQ-7 (8.8, 95%CI 4.7; 12.9, p < 0.001) with a response rate of 57.3%. Improvements observed in all other outcomes and high satisfaction (8.9/10, SD 1.8).
gemini-3.1-pro-preview__key_outcomes_fin: Of the 326 participants who started, 264 (81.0%) completed the intervention. Significant improvement on UIQ-7 (8.8, 95%CI 4.7; 12.9, p < 0.001) was observed, corresponding to a response rate of 57.3%. Improvements were also seen in all other outcomes, along with high satisfaction (8.9/10, SD 1.8). The study demonstrated the feasibility and safety of the completely remote DCP.
claude-sonnet-4-6__key_outcomes_findings: Of 326 participants who started the program, 264 (81.0%) completed the intervention. Significant improvement on UIQ-7 was observed (mean change 8.8, 95% CI 4.7–12.9, p < 0.001), corresponding to a response rate of 57.3%. Improvements were also observed in all secondary outcomes. High satisfaction was reported (mean 8.9/10, SD 1.8). The study concluded that the remote DCP with biofeedback is feasible and safe for reducing UI-related symptoms in a real-world setting.
